b' Money Stuff
Programming note: Money Stuff will be off for the rest of the week. Happy Thanksgiving!When I was an investment banker, one thing that I did

Programming note: Money Stuff will be off for the rest of the week. Happy Thanksgiving!

S&P

When I was an investment banker, one thing that I did was pitch convertible bond offerings to companies. A coverage banker \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0an investment banker whose job was to know companies and advise them on financial topics \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0would bring me to a meeting with the chief financial officer of one of her companies to talk about convertible bonds.\xc2\xa0And I would come in and\xc2\xa0say \xe2\x80\x9cthe convertible bond market is hot, you should do one, we think you could raise $200 million of 5-year convertibles and pay 2% interest with a 30% conversion premium.\xe2\x80\x9d Most of the time, their eyes would glaze over and they\xe2\x80\x99d nod politely and say \xe2\x80\x9cthanks for that, very interesting\xe2\x80\x9d and move on with their day and not do a convertible. But sometimes they would say \xe2\x80\x9coh yes we do need $200 million and those terms sound attractive, let\xe2\x80\x99s do it next week,\xe2\x80\x9d and then we\xe2\x80\x99d do some business and earn some fees. And that is life as a product banker.

I was not, in those conversations, making a firm bid for those convertible bonds. That is mostly not how it works. [1] The company was not going to sell those bonds to me; it was going to sell them to the market, to convertible arbitrage hedge funds and other investors who buy convertibles. (We would advise the company and market and underwrite the bonds.) The terms the company would get \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the size and interest rate and conversion premium \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0would depend on what those investors were willing to take. I made no promises. I just had a page in a pitchbook with an estimate of what the market might want.

That estimate was cooked up back at the office the day before,\xc2\xa0using a convertible bond pricing model and somewhere between \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0let\xe2\x80\x99s say two and 45 minutes of banker time? Sometimes we would pitch an important client who might really want to do a convertible, and so there\xe2\x80\x99d be some discussion about market appetite and the company\xe2\x80\x99s credit and its financing needs and the parameters to use in the pricing model; senior people would get involved to give their informed view of what the market might think, and we\xe2\x80\x99d reach a consensus on aggressive-but-achievable terms to show the client. Other times I\xe2\x80\x99d be flying to see five random companies in an afternoon, small companies with no credit ratings who had never done a convertible and never would. Then an analyst would chuck some guesses into the model, get some numbers out, and type them into a pitch book. Sometimes those guesses would be wrong. This did not matter, though, because the client\xe2\x80\x99s eyes would glaze over and they\xe2\x80\x99d throw the pitch book in the garbage and that would be that.

But every once in a long while one of\xc2\xa0those pitches would work, and the client would say \xe2\x80\x9cyes let\xe2\x80\x99s do it next week,\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0and then we\xe2\x80\x99d all sit down and be like \xe2\x80\x9cwait can this company actually do the thing we pitched?\xe2\x80\x9d There is the possibility of adverse selection here: If you pitch very attractive terms to a company that could not actually get those terms (because your analyst priced it up in two minutes assuming it was a good credit, but actually it was a bad credit), then the company is more likely to say \xe2\x80\x9cthat sounds great I want that,\xe2\x80\x9d and then you are on the hook to do the deal.

Of course we were not really on the hook. We could go back to the company and say \xe2\x80\x9chey, I know that in my pitch book I said you could raise a $200 million convertible with a 2% interest rate and a 30% conversion premium, but we actually gave that no thought at all, and now we have given it some thought and the correct numbers are $100 million, 4% and 20%.\xe2\x80\x9d That\xe2\x80\x99s totally allowed! We were not held to the terms we pitched; we had no binding contract with the company to deliver it those terms. But if we did that:

  • The company\xe2\x80\x99s CFO would give us a disappointed look, and
  • The coverage investment banker would give us a disappointed look, because we made her look bad in front of the client.

Also the company might change its mind about doing a convertible (quite reasonably!) and we wouldn\xe2\x80\x99t get a fee, and the coverage banker might stop bringing me to pitch meetings (quite reasonably!) and I would miss out on future business. But realistically the biggest and most immediate problem was the disappointed looks. This would mostly happen over phone calls, so you had to imagine the disappointed looks, but that was not hard to do.

I can\xe2\x80\x99t really tell you what the answer is here; \xe2\x80\x9chope the company changes its mind,\xe2\x80\x9d\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x9chope market conditions get better so you can do the deal you pitched\xe2\x80\x9d and \xe2\x80\x9chope market conditions get worse so\xc2\xa0you have an excuse for doing a worse deal\xe2\x80\x9d are the main\xc2\xa0possibilities. One way to be\xc2\xa0good at this sort of banking would be to get everything right, so that every pitch contains realistic terms, but that probably takes more time and effort than it\xe2\x80\x99s worth. The \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0harder? \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0way to be good at this sort of banking is to be a person who can get on a call with a client and say \xe2\x80\x9cthat good thing we promised you, we can\xe2\x80\x99t do it, but we can do this other worse thing,\xe2\x80\x9d and have the client walk away feeling good about it. That is charm, or whatever it is.

Anyway all of this is hypothetical and I never got anything wrong, but imagine if I did.

Separately here is a US Securities and Exchange Commission enforcement action from last week against S&P Global Ratings:

The Securities and Exchange Commission today charged S&P Global Ratings, a nationally recognized statistical rating organization (NRSRO) registered with the Commission, with violating conflict of interest rules designed to prevent sales and marketing considerations from influencing credit ratings.

The SEC\xe2\x80\x99s order finds that an issuer engaged S&P to rate a jumbo residential mortgage backed security transaction in July 2017. Over a five-day period in August 2017, S&P commercial employees\xe2\x80\x94employees responsible for managing the relationship with the issuer\xe2\x80\x94on several occasions attempted to pressure the S&P analytical employees\xe2\x80\x94employees responsible for evaluating and assigning the rating\xe2\x80\x94to rate the transaction consistent with preliminary feedback the analytical employees had given the customer that turned out to include a calculation error. Despite sending the communications through the compliance department as required by S&P\xe2\x80\x99s policies and procedures at that time, some emails sent by the S&P commercial employees to the S&P analytical team contained statements reflecting sales and marketing considerations. The order finds that, as a result of the content, urgent nature, high volume, and compressed timing of the communications, the S&P commercial employees became participants in the rating process during a time when they were influenced by sales and marketing considerations.

S&P initially told the customer\xc2\xa0that it would probably get a good rating. Then it discovered that it had made a calculation error, and the rating would be worse than it had \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0not promised, but given \xe2\x80\x9cpreliminary feedback\xe2\x80\x9d about anyway. The customer was, understandably, annoyed, and called the \xe2\x80\x9cS&P commercial employees\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the coverage salespeople \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0to yell at them. \xe2\x80\x9cThe S&P commercial employees stated to other commercial team members, at that time, that the issuer had threatened that, if S&P\xe2\x80\x99s \xe2\x80\x98ratings were changing, [S&P was] off the deal\xe2\x80\x99 and that, \xe2\x80\x98by removing [S&P] from the deal, [the issuer] was also ending the relationship in RMBS indefinitely and would also drop all [S&P] servicer and originator rankings.\xe2\x80\x99\xe2\x80\x9d The commercial employees, uh, conveyed this displeasure to the analytical team. The analytical team was chagrined, and\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x9cS&P analytical employees, working late on the night before the preliminary rating committee meeting,\xe2\x80\x9d maybe bent the requirements a bit to get to the client-friendly result they had previously promised.

And yes, look, if you make a mistake in your model, and as a result of that mistake you tell an issuer that you\xe2\x80\x99ll probably rate their bonds AAA, and then they hire you to rate the bonds, and you catch the mistake and realize that you can only rate the bonds AA, and the issuer gives you a disappointed look, and the relationship person gives you a disappointed look, and the issuer says things like \xe2\x80\x9cwell I won\xe2\x80\x99t be doing business with you anymore,\xe2\x80\x9d you could just say \xe2\x80\x9clook, sorry, we made a mistake, but the rules are the rules and we can\xe2\x80\x99t give you a AAA rating based on a mistake.\xe2\x80\x9d A credit rating is meant to reflect the credit quality of the bonds, not the nuances of the relationship between the ratings agency and the client. And yet! You try telling the client \xe2\x80\x9cwe made a mistake but we can\xe2\x80\x99t do anything about it now.\xe2\x80\x9d Think of the look on their faces.

Carbon accounting

Let us say, for simplicity, that you run a company that uses electric power, and you get half of your power from wind\xc2\xa0and half of your power from coal. The coal power you use puts one ton of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere per year. The wind power you use puts zero tons of carbon dioxide into the atmosphere;\xc2\xa0it is completely clean. The question is: How much total carbon emissions are you responsible for?

You might say: Well, one ton of carbon from the coal, plus zero tons from the wind, equals one ton. But we talked the other day about the financial services specialty of ESG Consultant But Evil, where you advise companies on how to reduce their \xe2\x80\x9ccarbon emissions\xe2\x80\x9d (a matter of accounting convention)\xc2\xa0without actually reducing their carbon emissions (a matter of actual physical emissions into the atmosphere). And as an accounting convention , you can improve on this math. Here is some better math:

  1. Your coal power adds one ton of carbon to the atmosphere.
  2. Your wind power subtracts one ton of carbon from the atmosphere, because if you weren\xe2\x80\x99t using the wind power you\xe2\x80\x99d be using coal, which would add one ton of carbon emissions, and you\xe2\x80\x99re not doing that. So you can count the ton of carbon that you\xe2\x80\x99re not using as a reduction of emissions.
  3. So your net carbon emissions are zero.

I swear this is a real thing, or almost. Here\xe2\x80\x99s a Bloomberg article titled \xe2\x80\x9c Junk Carbon Offsets Are What Make These Big Companies \xe2\x80\x98Carbon Neutral\xe2\x80\x99 \xe2\x80\x9d:

Airlines, online retailers, industrial firms and energy producers now rely heavily on the cheapest and most suspect type of offset \xe2\x80\x94 those tied to renewable-energy projects.

Most of these renewable-energy offset purchases are not credible, according to Julio Friedmann, chief scientist at consultancy Carbon Direct and one of six researchers who reviewed the data. \xe2\x80\x9cI would consider these to be low-quality credits that did not avoid or reduce greenhouse-gas emissions,\xe2\x80\x9d he said.

Purchasing credits tied to support of solar or wind projects sounds good for the climate. But experts consider these offsets largely bogus. The issue is timing: many renewable offsets came into being just as solar and wind power established themselves as the cheapest source of energy in most countries. Selling offsets for small sums as a way to support the economics of renewables doesn\xe2\x80\x99t provide any real benefit if it\xe2\x80\x99s already cheaper than building new coal or gas power plants.

That\xe2\x80\x99s the basis on which these offsets are generated: additional support for something clean is assumed to displace a dirtier alternative. Offsets built on firmer footing \xe2\x80\x94 and costing far more money \xe2\x80\x94 now exist from a nascent industry that directly removes carbon dioxide from the air.

The idea is that if you support a renewable project \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0for instance by committing in advance to pay for some power produced by a proposed renewable project, so that it can get financing to get built \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0then you are reducing emissions and should be rewarded. But that can end up looking a lot like double-counting, where your use of renewables doesn\xe2\x80\x99t add to your emissions but also, somehow, subtracts from them.

FTX investigation

As of about three weeks ago, a plausible\xc2\xa0model of FTX Trading Ltd. and its affiliated trading firm Alameda Research\xc2\xa0would have been:

  1. FTX and Alameda make\xc2\xa0a lot of money.
  2. Is there something fishy about that?

The standard sorts of theories would be along the lines of \xe2\x80\x9cAlameda and FTX supposedly operated independently, but they seemed to all be friends and live together, so\xc2\xa0maybe Alameda had privileged access to FTX\xe2\x80\x99s order flow and so could make unusually profitable trades,\xe2\x80\x9d something like that. In fact, though, the story seems to have been more like:

  1. Alameda actually lost a ton of money.
  2. Then it used FTX customer money to cover its losses.

If you believed that Alameda was suspiciously\xc2\xa0profitable, and you pursued that hunch, would that have led you to the truth, which is that Alameda was suspiciously unprofitable ? Maybe? Some of your intuitions \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x9cthe boundaries between FTX and Alameda are more porous than they claim\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0would have served you well in uncovering the truth. Others \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0\xe2\x80\x9cAlameda makes a lot of money trading\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0would not have.

Similarly, if you believed that FTX was violating the Bank Secrecy Act, would that have pointed you to the real problems? Apparently not :

The US Attorney\xe2\x80\x99s Office for the Southern District of New York, led by Damian Williams, spent several months working on a sweeping examination of crypto currency platforms with US and offshore arms and had started poking into FTX\xe2\x80\x99s massive exchange operations, according to people familiar with the investigation.

The focus was on compliance with the Bank Secrecy Act, the people said. Authorities have used the law, requiring financial institutions take steps to prevent money laundering and terrorism financing, to go after crypto platforms that allegedly falsely claimed that they don\xe2\x80\x99t serve US customers. Bahamas-based FTX operated one of the world\xe2\x80\x99s largest international crypto exchanges, as well as a separate and much more limited venue called FTX US that said it complies with the act.

It\xe2\x80\x99s unclear whether prosecutors in Manhattan reached any conclusion in their probe before FTX -- valued at nearly $32 billion in a January financing -- collapsed, sending the crypto market into a dive and raising questions about the accuracy of its pledges to safeguard customer assets. That put the federal investigation into a new trajectory, the people said.

Of all the illegal things an offshore crypto exchange could do, accepting deposits from US customers is the most obvious one to worry about. If you were an ambitious prosecutor and you called up FTX and said \xe2\x80\x9cwe need you to prove that you don\xe2\x80\x99t accept US customers,\xe2\x80\x9d would you have learned anything interesting about FTX\xe2\x80\x99s other problems? One thing that we have all learned recently is that FTX\xe2\x80\x99s record-keeping was a mess, in ways that might have caused or at least encouraged the misuse of customer funds. If you called up FTX and said \xe2\x80\x9cprove that you don\xe2\x80\x99t accept US customers\xe2\x80\x9d and FTX said to you \xe2\x80\x9chmm, interesting, you want a list of our customers? Not sure we have that anywhere,\xe2\x80\x9d that might have raised additional, non-Bank-Secrecy-Act-related red flags. \xe2\x80\x9cWait now that I have you here, prove that you actually have your customers\xe2\x80\x99 money,\xe2\x80\x9d would have been a reasonable follow-up question, when you saw the disarray of FTX\xe2\x80\x99s records.

On the other hand, if you are a New York federal prosecutor, and you can\xe2\x80\x99t find a Bank Secrecy Act violation, maybe you don\xe2\x80\x99t care about the rest? If FTX wasn\xe2\x80\x99t handling money for US customers then why should a US prosecutor care if it was also misusing the money? I am pretty sure we\xe2\x80\x99ll get answers to those questions soon enough.

Bain

Speaking of investigations that failed to find problems at FTX :

Bain & Co. was among consulting firms that helped conduct due diligence for Tiger Global Management\xe2\x80\x99s investment in now-defunct crypto exchange FTX, according to people familiar with the matter.

Tiger Global, which pays Bain more than $100 million a year to research private companies, has now written down its $38 million FTX stake to zero, the people said. Sam Bankman-Fried\xe2\x80\x99s oversight of a vast web of FTX-linked entities was one of the risks highlighted during the due-diligence process, but the money manager still believed it was a sound investment at the time, one of the people said. \xe2\x80\xa6

Tiger Global\xe2\x80\x99s FTX investment was a tiny portion of its $12.7 billion Private Investment Partners 15 fund. The firm first backed FTX in October 2021, when the crypto exchange was valued at $25 billion, and again in January at a $32.5 billion valuation, according to PitchBook data.

The FTX bet was one of 358 venture capital investments Tiger Global made last year, and one of 290 so far in 2022, according to PitchBook.

Tiger Global called Bain and was like \xe2\x80\x9chey guys, drop everything, we are thinking of investing 0.3% of our fund in a giant crypto exchange that makes a billion dollars a year and has Tom Brady as a spokesman, do you think that\xe2\x80\x99s a good idea,\xe2\x80\x9d and Bain dropped everything to investigate FTX, and then 20 minutes later Tiger Global called back and was like \xe2\x80\x9chey guys, drop everything, we are thinking of investing 0.4% of our fund in a pre-revenue dog treat delivery service,\xe2\x80\x9d and Bain was like \xe2\x80\x9ceh I\xe2\x80\x99m sure FTX is fine, you said dog treat delivery?\xe2\x80\x9d Tiger Global\xc2\xa0closed one investment a day last year! They famously bought absolutely everything ! This one made money and had Tom Brady! It probably was not a due diligence priority.

In general I am pretty sympathetic to the venture capitalists who invested in FTX. I wrote last week :

I suppose FTX is a failure of venture capitalist due diligence, but it\xe2\x80\x99s an odd kind. The usual VC due diligence failure is, like, you back an entrepreneur who promises a futuristic product, and the product doesn\xe2\x80\x99t work. FTX worked fine: People liked its technology, and it seems to have made money. The problem was in its balance sheet, which was full of snakes, and its governance, which put all the snakes there. Ideally the venture capitalists would have spotted that in due diligence, but the typical VC company has a very simple balance sheet and terrible governance, so it is sort of understandable that they sailed right by those problems.

But Tiger Global\xe2\x80\x99s whole thing was that they moved faster, did less due diligence and were more founder-friendly than anyone else. The good news is that they were also more diversified than everyone else, more of an index fund of private tech companies than a handful of concentrated bets that they really believed in. They got this one wrong, but so did a lot of people who probably put more time into it.

People are worried about crypto market liquidity

Oh man :

The wipeout of Sam Bankman-Fried\xe2\x80\x99s crypto empire, including its crown jewel FTX exchange and sister trading desk Alameda Research, is helping to reduce liquidity across the crypto market.

The decline has been dubbed the \xe2\x80\x9cAlameda Gap\xe2\x80\x9d by blockchain-data firm Kaiko, named for the trading group at the center of the storm which is closing its books. Plunges in liquidity usually come during periods of volatility as trading shops pull bids and asks from their order books to better regulate risks, Kaiko noted in a Nov. 17 newsletter.

\xe2\x80\x9cThe Alameda Gap in liquidity could be here to stay, at least in the short term,\xe2\x80\x9d wrote the analysts.

These market makers fulfill an especially valuable role in the liquidity-sensitive crypto space by buying and selling coins. But as they turn more conservative in times of crisis, that results in thinner liquidity where users have greater difficulty buying or selling an asset, which in turn makes the market more volatile, said Hank Huang, chief technology officer at Kronos Research.

\xe2\x80\x9cMarket makers are shutting off or getting funds into a safe place first,\xe2\x80\x9d added Tiantian Kullander, co-founder of Singapore-based crypto trading platform Amber Group, adding that they\xe2\x80\x99re \xe2\x80\x9cawaiting the storm to pass.\xe2\x80\x9d

One simple model of market making is that you buy a bunch of a thing as the price goes down and sell it as the price goes up; if the price is volatile but more or less mean-reverting then you make a lot of money buying low and selling high. If you buy a bunch as it goes down and then it goes down more and you buy more and it goes down more and you buy more and it goes down more and you buy more and it goes to zero and you have all of it, then that\xe2\x80\x99s bad, for you. But you have provided a valuable service, to your customers.

One simple (but speculative) model of how Alameda blew up is that it might have provided that valuable service during the blowup of the Luna token. [2] If you are a market maker in crypto in November 2022, you might look at that example and say, you know what, I\xe2\x80\x99m going to take the rest of the year off, spend some quality time with my family, try not to catch any falling knives for a bit.

Elsewhere in knife catching

Who should be the lender of last resort in crypto? If some crypto project runs into liquidity trouble, who should bail it out? I think the obvious answer (besides \xe2\x80\x9cno one\xe2\x80\x9d!) is something along the lines of \xe2\x80\x9ca consortium of people who have made a lot of money in crypto\xe2\x80\x9d: Bailing out troubled crypto projects is good for the value of crypto , and people who have a lot of crypto \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and who make money from crypto enthusiasm \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0have the most incentive to keep up the value of crypto. So for a while this summer, the lender of last resort in crypto was effectively Sam Bankman-Fried, who was a billionaire operator of a crypto exchange and trading firm. This made sense: Propping up other crypto firms was good for him, in expectation, though as it turns out it probably cost him too much money. So he is no longer available to serve as the lender of last resort, and now Changpeng \xe2\x80\x9cCZ\xe2\x80\x9d Zhao, who runs Binance, the biggest crypto exchange, wants to set up an \xe2\x80\x9cindustry recovery fund\xe2\x80\x9d to take over the role. Fine.

There is a problem with this, though, which is that if crypto crashes hard enough, the people in crypto probably can\xe2\x80\x99t bail it out. Loosely speaking bailing out crypto projects requires a lot of dollars, and if you have billions of dollars\xe2\x80\x99 worth of crypto and crypto crashes hard enough, you won't have enough dollars to do much good. What you really want is some external source of funds to backstop crypto, so that if everyone tries to take money out of crypto someone else is putting money back in. Ideally you find some government actor and say \xe2\x80\x9cyou can make crypto safer, more useful and more regulated; by the way part of making it safer is backstopping the price.\xe2\x80\x9d

Here\xe2\x80\x99s this :

Binance Chief Executive Officer Changpeng \xe2\x80\x9cCZ\xe2\x80\x9d Zhao and several deputies met with investors in Abu Dhabi last week in an effort to raise cash for a crypto industry recovery fund, according to people familiar with the matter.

Zhao and his team held meetings with potential backers last week, including with entities affiliated with United Arab Emirates National Security Adviser Sheikh Tahnoon Bin Zayed, who oversees a large financial empire, said the people, who spoke on the condition of anonymity because the talks were private. \xe2\x80\xa6

Zhao, who moved to Dubai last year and has built close ties with the UAE leadership, is casting himself as the crypto world\xe2\x80\x99s rescuer-in-chief after the spectacular downfall of Sam Bankman-Fried. After FTX\xe2\x80\x99s bankruptcy, Zhao tweeted that Binance would form a recovery fund \xe2\x80\x9cto help projects who are otherwise strong, but in a liquidity crisis.\xe2\x80\x9d

\xe2\x80\x9cThere are still players with very strong financials and we should band together to try to help the projects in need, especially if it\xe2\x80\x99s only financial need,\xe2\x80\x9d he said on the sidelines of Abu Dhabi Finance Week last Wednesday.

The Binance CEO, who also spoke at the Milken Institute\xe2\x80\x99s Abu Dhabi conference, had said there was significant investor interest in the industry recovery fund and that he expects to finalize commitments in the coming weeks. His conversations in the region also included proposals to engage with Middle Eastern regulators and boost crypto adoption, the people said.

How\xe2\x80\x99s GameStop doing?

You can just do this now; this is allowed again :

Billionaire investor Carl Icahn began shorting GameStop Corp. during the height of the meme-stock frenzy around January 2021 and still holds a large position in the video-game retailer, according to people familiar with the matter.

Icahn started building the short when GameStop was trading near its peak of $483 per share and still holds a large bet against the retailer\xe2\x80\x99s shares, said the people, asking not to be identified because the matter is private. The investor, who has added to his position from time to time, is betting that GameStop\xe2\x80\x99s stock isn\xe2\x80\x99t trading on its fundamentals and will continue to fall, the people said. \xe2\x80\xa6

The early response to Icahn\xe2\x80\x99s short on social media was relatively measured. News of the wager was shared on Reddit in at least two threads, including in the popular WallStreetBets forum, garnering more than 250 comments as of 9:54 a.m. in Singapore. That pales in comparison to the thousands of responses to GameStop posts during the height of meme mania.

The stock wasn\xe2\x80\x99t trending on social media platforms as it did in early September, when news emerged of its partnership with Sam Bankman-Fried\xe2\x80\x99s now bankrupt FTX US cryptocurrency exchange.

It was up about 3% at noon today. For a while, \xe2\x80\x9cbillionaire investment manager has short position in popular Reddit stock\xe2\x80\x9d was a way to make sure that the stock went up , and no stock was more short-squeeze-y than GameStop. To the point that we once discussed a\xc2\xa0trade where (1) you buy a meme-able stock, (2) you put out a research note on the stock that implies you are short, (3) you watch Reddit push the stock up and (4) profit. You could almost imagine Icahn doing this \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0short 10,000 shares, go long 1 million on swap, leak the short \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0except, not anymore. Now the excitement is gone, and no one is going to send GameStop to the moon just because a rich guy is short.

Everything is securities fraud

Now that Elon Musk has invited Donald Trump back on Twitter, is it securities fraud for Trump to tweet? Possibly? But also it might be securities fraud if he didn\xe2\x80\x99t tweet, and then started tweeting later? The problem\xc2\xa0is that Trump is committed to Truth Social, the social media ghost town of Trump Media & Technology Group, which has a long-delayed deal to go public by merging\xc2\xa0with Digital World Acquisition Corp. Semafor reports :

Trump has repeatedly said he's happy with his own Truth Social site and won\xe2\x80\x99t return, but given his relationship to both Twitter and the truth it's done little to dampen speculation that he\xe2\x80\x99d resume his dominant place on the central platform. ...

Since Trump is a core part of Truth Social\xe2\x80\x99s value, rejoining Twitter could create some potential legal complications, according to Eric Talley, a professor at Columbia Law School who specializes in corporate law.

In particular, if Trump repeatedly signals in public before a merger that he\xe2\x80\x99s never joining Twitter, then closes a SPAC deal and reneges, some shareholders could decide they were misled.

\xe2\x80\x9cIf it\xe2\x80\x99s going to look, later on, that he never had that intention\xe2\x80\x9d of remaining off Twitter \xe2\x80\x9cbut he just wanted to convince people that they should go ahead and close [the SPAC deal] that\xe2\x80\x99s kind of a textbook securities fraud lawsuit,\xe2\x80\x9d Talley said.

If Trump does decide to rejoin Twitter without incident, his deal could affect how he uses the service. An SEC filing by TMTG notes that the former president \xe2\x80\x9cis generally obligated to make any social media post on TruthSocial and may not make the same post on another social media site for 6 hours\xe2\x80\x9d through at least June 22, 2023. That date is notably the exact same point in the 2016 cycle when Trump declared his presidential run.

My guess is that the Truth Social/DWAC deal is never going to close so all of this is moot. (Not investing advice! Or legal advice!) But if the deal does close, will Donald Trump somehow get sued for securities fraud for tweeting? I mean, absolutely.

This was a good experience for customers \xe2\x80\x94 it made betting on FTX more predictable and less risky \xe2\x80\x94 and possibly good for Alameda, if it bought stuff that went down and then the stuff went up again. But when the Luna token collapsed earlier this year, Alameda would (on this theory) have been buying tons of Luna all the way down, leaving it with a huge stash of worthless Luna and a big loss.\xe2\x80\x9d This Twitter thread fleshes out that speculation a bit.
